data.title: Lifecore granted Sector Weight rating at KeyBanc on growth trajectory
data.description:
KeyBanc Capital Markets has initiated the coverage of Lifecore Biomedical (NASDAQ:LFCR) with a Sector Weight recommendation, noting a balanced outlook for the contract drug manufacturer’s growth prospects.

Analyst Paul Knight argued that Lifecore’s (NASDAQ:LFCR) focus, the fill/finish side of the business, is one of the fastest growing areas in contract manufacturing and one benefiting from the growth of biologics and the GLP-1 class of weight loss drugs.

Knight added that the company has also divested its non-core business, and a senior executive from Novo Holdings, the investment arm of Novo Nordisk's (NVO) controlling investor, has joined as its new VP of operations.

Amid capacity constraints for GLP-1s and a trend towards reshoring, “we believe these dynamics place LFCR in a position to win significant business in the coming years,” KeyBanc wrote, adding that the stock also trades at a relatively modest EV/sales ratio of 3.4x of its FY27 projections.

However, despite recent M&A deals such as Novo’s (NVO) acquisition of Catalent’s fill/finish sites, the analyst argued that Lifecore (NASDAQ:LFCR) will need to secure new programs or step up its contractual signups to achieve a meaningful multiple expansion.

data.title: Wells Fargo’s Paul Christopher: Expect choppiness ahead, trim positions
data.description:
Wells Fargo Investment Institute’s head of Global Market Strategy, Paul Christopher, is warning investors to prepare for potential market weakness amid rising inflation expectations.

In an interview with CNBC, Christopher expressed concern about recent increases in longer-dated inflation expectations, noting that “five-year inflation swaps have now ticked to highs for the year.”

The strategist pointed to growing market speculation around the Federal Reserve’s four-to-three majority composition and its potential impact on monetary policy.

“We do observe in the markets that there is some movement higher in longer dated inflation expectations, which indicates that markets are willing to at least price a possibility that the four-to-three majority could be decisive for maybe much lower rates in an extended way that could bring back inflation,” he said.

Christopher highlighted the concentration risk in today’s equity markets, where 10 stocks account for approximately 40% of the S&P 500’s (SP500) market capitalization. This concentration has created a pattern where “soft data pushes most of the stocks maybe a little bit lower, but then you get a good earnings report from one of those top 10, and it drags the market up higher,” resulting in choppy market conditions despite occasional record highs.

Given these conditions, the Wells Fargo strategist believes equity markets could be approaching a near-term top.

“We think there is the potential for some choppiness to continue and maybe turn into some more pronounced weakness, maybe a pullback 5-7% perhaps even 10%,” he cautioned, while recommending investors begin trimming positions.

For portfolio positioning, Christopher suggests maintaining an overweight to information technology (XLK) while reducing exposure to communication services (XLC) due to concentration concerns.

“Recycle those proceeds perhaps into some fixed income as a sort of a parking place while we get through the next few weeks,” he said.

data.title: Mega Matrix files $2B shelf for Ethena stablecoin token push
data.description:
Mega Matrix (NYSE:MPU) filed for a $2 billion shelf registration with the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on Thursday to fund its new digital asset treasury (DAT) strategy.

The company aims to accumulate stablecoin governance tokens, especially Ethena’s ENA token, “to build the largest stablecoin governance token DAT company”.

“By building strategic positions, MPU gains both financial upside and a seat at the table where the future of money is being coded," the company added in its press release.

USDe, developed by Ethena, is supported by a combination of staked assets and liquid stablecoins like USDC and USDT, while its dollar peg is preserved through automated hedging in perpetual and futures markets, as per AInvest.

The move highlights the company’s pivot toward blockchain-driven financial solutions, with a particular focus on USDe- a delta-neutral

data.title: Carlyle Group raises $20B for secondary private equity assets
data.description:
Carlyle Group's (NASDAQ:CG) AlpInvest unit raised $20B for its global secondary strategies, with the final close of its AlpInvest Secondaries Fund VIII, which reached its $15B hard cap, the company said on Thursday. The capital raise represents a doubling of the company's investable capital in secondaries investments over its predecessor program. 

The fund is part of the firm's AlpInvest Secondaries Program VIII (ASP VIII). The total amount raised for the strategy includes $3.2B in co-investment commitments and $2B for private wealth vehicles investing alongside ASP VIII.

ASP VIII focuses on acquiring exposure to existing private equity assets by providing a range of liquidity solutions to general partners and limited partners, including the acquisition of fund interests, continuation funds, and other GP-centered solutions, Carlyle (NASDAQ:CG) said.

"This fundraise is not only a milestone for our secondaries strategy, but also a validation of the integrated platform we've built across Secondaries and Portfolio Finance,"  said Michael Hacker, partner and global head of Portfolio Finance at Carlyle AlpInvest. "With over $24 billion raised for liquidity solutions across our platform, we are well positioned to provide private equity sponsors and investors with a comprehensive toolkit of liquidity and capital solutions."

Carlyle (NASDAQ:CG) stock climbed 2.1% in midafternoon trading.

Carlyle AlpInvest has committed more than $40B to more than 245 transactions across secondaries and portfolio finance in the past 20 year. 

The global private equity investor also recently raised more than $4B for its portfolio finance strategies.

data.title: Ciena targets 17% revenue growth in 2026 with accelerating AI-driven demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ciena Corporation (CIEN) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gary Smith opened the call by welcoming Marc Graff as the new CFO, highlighting strong Q3 results and strategic direction. Smith stated, "Q3 '25 revenue was $1.22 billion, above the top end of our guidance. Importantly, as we focus on driving increased profitability, we delivered quarterly adjusted EPS of $0.67, up 60% sequentially and 91% year-over-year." He emphasized continued robust demand from both Cloud Provider and Service Provider segments, noting that two customers each contributed 10% of revenue, including a global cloud provider and a Tier 1 service provider.
  • Smith reported record Q3 orders, stating, "our Q3 order book...set a new quarterly record for us," and attributed this surge to the network's critical role in supporting AI infrastructure. He described two industry-first wins: a dedicated AI infrastructure project for a North American cloud provider involving RLS and WaveLogic 6 Nano 800-gig ZR pluggables, and a data center management solution (DCOM) co-developed with a hyperscaler. Initial shipments for the AI interconnect project have begun and are expected "to ramp to hundreds of millions of dollars over the next several quarters."
  • Smith also forecasted significant growth in the Interconnects portfolio, indicating, "we are on track to meet our expectations to at least double revenue year-over-year for our Interconnects portfolio in 2025. We now also believe that we're likely to be in a position to at least double and more our Interconnects revenue again in FY '26."
  • He announced a strategic shift to prioritize investment in Coherent Optical Systems, Interconnects, Coherent Routing, and innovative solutions like DCOM, stating, "we will be redirecting additional R&D investment into these technologies and away from our residential broadband access portfolio."
  • CFO Marc Graff stated, "Revenue of $1.22 billion exceeded the high end of our guidance, up 8% sequentially and nearly 30% year-over-year," and pointed to strong performance in RLS optical products and Routers and Switches. Graff outlined his initial focus on gross margin improvement, working capital management, and capital allocation. He added, "We delivered adjusted operating margin of 10.7%, up 270 basis points year-on-year...adjusted net income was $96 million and adjusted EPS was $0.67, up 91% year-on-year."

Outlook

  • For fiscal Q4, Graff provided guidance of revenue in a range of $1.24 billion to $1.32 billion, with adjusted gross margins between 42% and 43%. He stated, "We believe fiscal Q2 marked the floor for gross margins, and we expect improving trends over the next several quarters."
  • Looking ahead, management expects "approximately 17% year-on-year growth in fiscal 2026, similar to what we're currently projecting in fiscal '25," with an initial gross margin estimate of 43%, plus or minus 1 point. Operating expense is projected to be flat year-over-year at approximately $1.5 billion.
  • Ciena is accelerating its timeline for operating margin targets, with Graff noting, "we now believe that we will accelerate our longer-term goal of 15% to 16% operating margin by 1 year from 2027 to 2026, driven by increased operating leverage and improving gross margins."

Financial Results

  • Q3 revenue was $1.22 billion, adjusted gross margin reached 41.9%, and adjusted operating expense was $380 million, with operating margin at 10.7%. Adjusted net income was $96 million and adjusted EPS was $0.67.
  • Ciena generated $174 million in cash from operations, and free cash flow margin was 11%. The company ended the quarter with approximately $1.4 billion in cash and investments, and repurchased 1 million shares for $81.8 million in Q3.
  • The company expects to record a noncash charge in Q4 against in-process R&D of approximately $90 million due to the strategic shift, and a Q4 restructuring expense of approximately $20 million related to a reduction in headcount of about 4% to 5% of the workforce.

Q&A

  • George Notter, Wolfe Research: Asked about industry consolidation and gross margin prospects. Smith responded, "I think the structure of the industry has improved considerably over the last few years. And that does play through to our expectation. It's a confluence of elements around our improving gross margin."
  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: Inquired about the neo-scaler opportunity and growth composition. Smith described neo-scalers as a "net incremental opportunity" that will scale over time and expects them to play a more significant role in overall growth.
  • Meta Marshall, Morgan Stanley: Asked about gross margin drivers. Graff explained, "we should be seeing some tailwinds from improving ramps of those products as they get cost reduced just through scale," and noted a 20% sequential increase in plug shipments.
  • Ruben Roy, Stifel: Sought clarification on the residential broadband strategy. Smith clarified, "we're going to continue to support our broadband initiatives there. We are just really not going to put the longer-term road map for that in place."
  • Simon Leopold, Raymond James: Asked about the DCI opportunity and routing/switching performance. Smith described dedicated training networks as "super high speed, low latency...using our RLS and the 800-gig plugs at scale."
  • Tim Long, Barclays: Queried about the Interconnects business doubling and customer breadth. McFeely responded, "the number of customers here measures in the 10s, multiple 10s."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were broadly positive, focusing on Ciena's backlog strength, AI-related growth, gross margin expansion, and strategic wins. Their tone reflected interest in sustainability and composition of future growth, with some probing on margin improvements and new market opportunities.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, especially in prepared remarks. Graff repeatedly referenced "confidence" and "visibility" into future demand, and Smith emphasized the durability of AI-driven networking demand. In the Q&A, management remained clear and assertive, offering detailed explanations and affirming guidance upgrades.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained optimistic but pressed further on the scale and sustainability of AI-driven growth, while management showed increased confidence and provided more detailed long-term projections.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance for Q4 increased from the prior quarter, with a higher sequential revenue and gross margin outlook. Management now projects 17% revenue growth for fiscal 2026, accelerating its targets for operating margin improvement by one year.
  • Strategic focus has shifted further away from residential broadband towards AI-centric technologies, with new R&D allocation and an announced workforce reduction.
  • Analysts' questions shifted more heavily towards the composition and durability of AI-driven demand, the neo-scaler market, and specifics of large project wins.
  • Management's tone has grown more confident, especially regarding margin improvement, competitive positioning, and long-term growth visibility, compared to a more cautious but optimistic approach last quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted the fluidity of the tariff environment but expects impacts to be immaterial moving forward. Graff stated, "We continue to work closely with our supply chain and customers to monitor and respond to any changes in the tariff environment."
  • A noncash R&D charge and restructuring costs are expected in Q4 as a result of the strategic investment shift.
  • Supply chain constraints persist, but management believes investments made will enable further scaling.
  • Analysts questioned sustainability of gross margin improvements amid new product introductions and changing mix, but management expressed confidence in cost reductions and operational efficiencies.

Final Takeaway

Ciena's third quarter results highlight strong AI-driven demand fueling both revenue and margin expansion, with record orders and large-scale wins in AI infrastructure and data center management. The company is shifting strategic investment toward high-speed optical and interconnect solutions, projecting 17% revenue growth in fiscal 2026 while advancing its operating margin targets. Management's confidence is underpinned by a record order backlog, deepening cloud and neo-scaler relationships, and robust service provider demand, positioning Ciena for sustained growth in a rapidly evolving network infrastructure market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Salesforce, Figma fall on quarterly results as other enterprise software firms show mixed action
data.description:
Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) was down 5% by early afternoon trading following guidance that failed to live up to the investor hype around its artificial intelligence applications.

Other enterprise software firms were also declining following the release of quarterly results. Figma (NYSE:FIG), a cloud-based design platform that competes with companies like Adobe (NASDAQ:ADBE) and Canva, had plummeted 18% by early afternoon trading.

This occurred despite Figma's second quarter revenue increasing 41% year over year and its third quarter outlook demonstrating 33% growth. Its full-year outlook calls for revenue increasing by 37% year over year. However, these forecasts were merely in line with consensus estimates.

Figma launched its initial public offering in late July, with shares surging to more than $120 in one day compared to its original pricing of $33 per share. Share prices had since moderated in the $60s prior to today's tumble.

Adobe shares had slid 2%.

Asana (NYSE:ASAN), which offers a work management platform, had ticked up 4% following its second quarter financial results and outlook. 

ServiceNow (NOW) was down 2%, Atlassian (TEAM) had fallen 1.7% and monday.com (MNDY) had edged down 2.2%. SAP (SAP) had inched up 0.3%.

Couchbase (NASDAQ:BASE), which issued second quarter results on Wednesday as well, was relatively static. Palantir (PLTR) was also flat.

data.title: Carlyle unit raises $20B for private equity secondaries strategy
data.description:
Carlyle Group (NASDAQ:CG) said on Thursday that its unit, Carlyle AlpInvest, has raised $20B for its global private equity secondaries strategy.

The AlpInvest Secondaries Fund VIII, which reached its $15B hard cap, is focused on acquiring exposure to existing private equity assets.

The total amount raised includes $3.2B in co-investment commitments, as well as $2B for private wealth vehicles investing alongside the fund.

Over 325 new and existing investors committed capital to the fund.

The secondary market for private equity funds involves buying and selling of pre-existing investor commitments to private equity and other alternative investment funds or the underlying private equity assets.

data.title: Wall Street trades higher ahead of Friday's payrolls report
data.description:
Wall Street trades higher on Thursday as investors weigh fresh jobs data for clues on the labor market’s strength.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is +0.5%, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is +0.4%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) is +0.4%.

On a sector-by-sector basis, eight of the 11 S&P segments are trading in positive territory, led by Consumer Discretionary and Energy. At the other end of the spectrum, Utilities has lagged the most on the session. 

In the bond market, yields are under some slight pressure as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) has dipped 4 basis points to 4.17%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) had pushed down 2 basis points to 3.59%.

On the economic front, job cuts climbed 39% in August, boosting the YTD total higher than all of 2024. Also, U.S. private sector employment continued softening, slightly more than expected, in August. 

Initial jobless claims rose slightly more than expected in the past week. Productivity was revised up, and labor costs were revised lower in second Q2 prints. 

The U.S. goods and services trade deficit widened more than expected in July.

Additionally, the U.S. ISM Services Index rose to 52.0 in August from 50.1 in July, exceeding the 50.9 consensus, according to data released by the Institute for Supply Management

"The global bond selloff finally paused for breath yesterday, as weak U.S. data meant investors ramped up their expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year. The main catalyst was the JOLTS report for July, which showed that job openings fell to a 10-month low and exacerbated fears about a labor market slowdown," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said. 

On more of the political side, President Donald Trump on Wednesday urged the Supreme Court to quickly accept and rule on an appeal aiming to overturn lower court rulings that deemed most of his tariffs illegal.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) have surged 34.1% after the apparel maker said the advertising campaign starring actress Sydney Sweeney gave its brand and business a reset.

data.title: Bank of America to offer private markets investments to ultra-high-net-worth clients
data.description:
Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) Private Bank and Merrill Wealth Management, another BofA division, introduced a private market program called Alts Expanded Access for ultra-high-net-worth ("UHNW") clients with a net worth of $50M or more, the bank said on Thursday.

Key features of the Alts Expanded Access Program include:

  • Funds that are not broadly distributed and provide access to specialized opportunities in emerging themes, niche strategies and evolving sectors;
  • Client advisor or teams that help clients understand the process and provide access to fund manager materials; and
  • The ability for clients to conduct due diligence, make investment decisions, and invest directly with fund managers.

The program, to be available in fall 2025, is meant to complement the investment options available through Merrill's and Bank of America Private Bank's core Alternative Investments platform, which offers qualified investors ways to build an expanded allocation to alternatives as part of a diversified portfolio.

"Traditionally, private market alternatives were the domain of institutional investors, but as wealth building needs have evolved, we're seeing more clients seek non-traditional investments, fueled by market changes and the desire to diversify," said Mark Sutterlin, head of alternative investments for Merrill and Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) Private Bank.

data.title: Caleres outlines $15M annualized cost savings and integration of Stuart Weitzman amid tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Caleres Inc. (CAL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John Schmidt, President and CEO, reported, "Sales trends improved sequentially in both segments of our business, and we saw market share gains in both women's fashion footwear and in shoe chains." He emphasized that lead brands delivered sales growth, international sales increased by double digits, and Famous Footwear saw solid improvement in July, with that trend continuing into August.
  • Schmidt announced the completion of structural cost savings initiatives expected to deliver annualized savings of $15 million, with about half realized this year. He also detailed the completed acquisition of Stuart Weitzman, describing it as an iconic brand aligned with Caleres' strategic focus, and indicated that operational efficiencies and expense savings are anticipated following its integration.
  • Schmidt discussed ongoing efforts to mitigate tariff impacts, including working with factory partners and implementing moderate price increases, noting the lag between tariff enactment and mitigation effects.
  • Jack Calandra, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "For the second quarter, sales were $658.5 million, down 3.6%. Sales were lower in both Brand Portfolio and Famous, but the trend improved in both segments versus 1Q." Calandra noted, "We estimate that tariffs negatively impacted 2Q sales by $10 million due to order cancellations and delayed receipts that pushed sales into 3Q."

Outlook

  • The company is not providing annual guidance due to ongoing tariff uncertainty. Calandra shared, "For Famous, as mentioned earlier, comparable sales for August were a positive 1%, and August is the biggest month of the quarter. While we are pleased with our back-to-school results, we expect comparable sales in the largely nonpromotional months of September and October to be down low single digits."
  • For Brand Portfolio, August sales (excluding Stuart Weitzman) were up low single digits compared to last year. Calandra explained, "We expect Brand Portfolio 3Q gross margin, excluding Stuart Weitzman, to be down a similar amount to 2Q with improvement in the trend in 4Q as we realize more of the benefit of our mitigation strategies."
  • Additional structural cost savings are expected in 2026 and beyond, as the company continues to explore further efficiencies with the assistance of a consulting partner.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted earnings per share were $0.35.
  • Second-quarter sales were $658.5 million, a 3.6% year-over-year decline.
  • Consolidated gross margin was 43.4%, down 210 basis points from the prior year, with Brand Portfolio gross margin at 40.3% and Famous gross margin at 43.7%. The gross margin impact from tariffs was about 250 basis points, and markdown reserves accounted for about 120 basis points.
  • SG&A expenses rose to $269.7 million, 41% of sales, and operating earnings were $16 million with an operating margin of 2.4%.
  • Inventory at quarter end was $693 million, up 4.9% year-over-year, with increases in both segments. The company borrowed $120 million prior to quarter-end to complete the Stuart Weitzman acquisition.

Q&A

  • Ashley Owens, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Asked for details on Famous Footwear's August performance and drivers. Jack Calandra responded, "We saw by channel is in brick-and-mortar, we saw improved traffic and conversion with AURs basically flat. And then on the web... AURs were also higher there and conversion was flat." Schmidt added, "We did see the good effect of our product assortment shift... the Jordan piece continued to trend as we launched it all the way through back-to-school."
  • Owens asked about gross margin expectations. Schmidt replied, "We do believe that we've gotten through that promotional cycle... we don't have any plans to change our promotional cycle from last year. Over on the Brand Portfolio, we do think that as inventory becomes more aligned with sales, we're going to see less headwind on that inventory markdown piece."
  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport Research: Queried about Stuart Weitzman's financial impact. Liz Dunn said, "We're not providing that detail at this time. There's just a number of things that are still in flux... We will provide a breakout of Stuart through the end of the year." Calandra explained interest expense would reflect the $120 million borrowing at a 5.7-5.8% rate.
  • Kummetz followed up on Stuart Weitzman’s earnings contribution. Schmidt stated, "We're not getting that far through, that would be our goal. As we said, we were going through this transition period, which we expect to complete by the end of January."
  • Kummetz sought quantification of Brand Portfolio's order cancellations and delayed receipts. Calandra replied, "The split between what was canceled orders and what was delayed sales that we should recover in Q3 is about 50-50. So about $5 million of cancellations and about $5 million in delayed receipts, which we should benefit from in Q3."
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about consumer health and brand performance. Schmidt responded, "We're continuing to see our consumer want those highly demanded national brands... Some of our most newest and more elevated brands and products are growing faster."
  • Telsey asked about tariff mitigation tactics and future cost savings. Schmidt answered that the company is using selective price increases, negotiations with factory partners, and further structural cost savings. Calandra added, "We brought a partner in to help us with the integration of Stuart Weitzman... But we've also asked them to look more broadly at the company's cost structure to look for other structural opportunities."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment during Q&A was neutral to slightly cautious, focusing on gross margin pressures, promotional cycles, and the integration of Stuart Weitzman. Owens and Kummetz sought clarity on specific financial impacts and future expectations, without overt skepticism or strong negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and controlled tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience, ongoing mitigation strategies, and cost savings. During Q&A, responses were measured but candid about uncertainties, particularly around tariffs and the Stuart Weitzman integration. Schmidt frequently referenced ongoing efforts and future objectives, stating, "We are confident that executing our strategic plans will result in improved financial performance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more constructive, reflecting improved sequential trends, while analysts maintained a steady, fact-finding approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remains suspended due to tariff uncertainty in both quarters, but management provided additional color on quarterly expectations for Q3 and Q4 this time.
  • Structural cost savings initiatives progressed, with $15 million annualized savings completed versus the previous quarter's announcement.
  • The Stuart Weitzman acquisition was completed after quarter-end, shifting the strategic focus toward integration and synergy realization.
  • Market share gains in both women's fashion footwear and shoe chains continued, with lead brands and direct-to-consumer channels outperforming.
  • Gross margin pressure from tariffs and inventory markdowns persisted, but management expects mitigation strategies to show benefits in Q4.
  • Analyst focus shifted slightly from immediate inventory and tariff impacts to deeper questions on integration, profitability timelines, and cost management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Ongoing tariff uncertainty and its lagged impact on gross margins and sales remain significant risks. Calandra noted, "There is a lag between when the higher tariffs have taken effect and when these mitigating actions become effective."
  • Elevated inventory levels pose a risk, though management expects better alignment with sales trends going forward.
  • Delayed receipts and order cancellations affected sales, with $10 million in 2Q attributed to tariffs (split evenly between cancellations and delays).
  • The integration of Stuart Weitzman presents operational complexity, with management highlighting ongoing purchase accounting finalization and the need to move through acquired inventory.
  • Promotional intensity, particularly at Famous Footwear, could impact margins if consumer demand for elevated price points weakens.

Final Takeaway

Caleres highlighted sequential improvement in sales trends, successful market share gains, and the completion of the Stuart Weitzman acquisition as key developments in the second quarter. While gross margins remain pressured by tariffs and inventory markdowns, management underscored ongoing mitigation efforts and structural cost savings that are expected to yield $15 million in annualized benefits, with further efficiencies targeted for 2026. The company is not issuing annual guidance due to continued tariff uncertainty but provided a more detailed outlook for Q3 and Q4, signaling confidence in its ability to manage near-term headwinds while integrating new assets and pursuing additional cost reductions.
